author: @reypolice5231
text:
Just to let everyone know. Around here people abandon  their bikes. They end up at the police garage. Sit for years. There is 30 of them at just one police garage. They told me to come and take them away.

So you might find some bikes abandon at police station, if not try the next town.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an awesome tip mate - cheers

author: @reypolice5231
text:
The steering upgrade was a stroke of brilliance. So simple to do.

On the one I want to build for a senior that can't drive, I plan to have the Post are out not in for better leverage for her. If I am wrong I just put it the way he has it.
 I also want to maybe bend the handle bar out more for better leverage for a senior, also so if she bumps into something and rocks forward does not get poked in the chest with the handle bar.

I am also thinking of putting the tie rod way out front; for better leverage, and so that's not in the way to hit her either.

I want to hang the lights on the tie rod so the front lights move with the direction of the wheels.

I see he's got a solar panel there and that's a roof maybe, for rain and too much sun. It will charge batteries all day when there's Sun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like the plan mate - sounds good to me

author: @noanyobiseniss7462
text: Can Bamboo stand up to powder coating temps?

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Try it with a small piece and let me know please.

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
Power coating requires 350 degrees Fahrenheit.

I suspect if you sand the outside of the wood, it should Bond. 

But powder coating requires the metal to be electrically charged for the power to stick.

My other concern is that a 350°, it will carbonize the bamboo and or make it brittle. Stronger but brittle.

So take a piece of bamboo and stick it in your oven at 350° for about 15 minutes and see what happens, versus a piece of bamboo untreated. Does it make it stronger or does it make it more brittle?

You can do this by getting a bamboo chopstick from any Chinese restaurant.

If you're just trying to figure out how to paint it and don't care about powder coat, try dip. Dip comes in many colors and is about $125 a gallon. If you add polyurethane to the dip you just extended how much paint you got. The polyurethane makes it much tougher and makes it last longer.

The nice thing about dip is if you decide you don't like the color you can always just peel it off, of your car or anything you put it on. If you add polyurethane you need a heat gun to take it off. But no biggie just some heat from a hair dryer or heat gun and a little more work to get it off.

I do not know if the powder coat will actually go on with the heat gun or not?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have no idea mate - I like Steves answer though - try it and see

author: @deslomeslager
text: Can yuo use the solar panel next to you, and use it as a roof? And then leave out the batteries? I understand it will not run without sunlight, but for testing purposes it is great to know. Can you ride a quad on sunlight only? (maybe use a few super capacitors to store some extra energy when you are at a red light and need to go up hill later on).

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
One solar panel give on average 25 to 100 watts on the best day. Real expensive give more.

NASA put a fernel lens above the solar panel back in the seventies and It produced eight times as much power as without a fernel lens.

A fernel lens of that size is $50 or more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well it's certainly worth a try mate

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
I saw a video on the solar controller. They show that the old style controller was less effective/ efficient by 50% than the new one.

So you don't need a lot of panels, you need a better controller to charge the batteries more effectively.

replies.author: @deslomeslager
replies.text: @reypolice5231 This question is about using the DC output directly to the motor, not about storing in a battery. But yes: your comment does make sense on it's own.

author: @hommerdalor6301
text:
Hi Rob.
Free strings cut from platic bottles, then, the string is thermoretractable. If the joint came a little loose in the future, just blow hot air on it.
It begins shrinking from about 70c° to shrink more, raise the temp progressively, I tried up to 450C°, my hot air gun can't do more, théoricaly, the bottle can be reduced to the same diameter as the cork, I manage to go to 3cm. 
As an emergency repair, when I had to kwickly  replace our basement water pomp, in the middle of the night, as usual the "new pomp" had a different exhaust diameter, so I cut top and bottom of a bottle, shrinked it over the tubes, added some silicone when it was almost tight, and finished shrinking.
It's still in place and water tight.

replies.author: @reypolice5231
replies.text:
Good to know, that works.

Some silicon formulas have arsenic in them and other toxic chemicals. So you may want to replace it, if it's drinking water.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did think about it mate and I like the idea - I have certainly stored it for later use lol - cheers

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text: Thanks for your reactions, mates, the water is going straight in the sewage.
